question_keywords: plastics only; what does plastics only mean; what can go in this bin; accepted items; what this bin accepts
user_question: What does plastics-only mean?
short_answer: Only eligible plastic items belong in this bin.
full_chatbot_answer: Plastics-only means this bin is not a general recycling bin. Only eligible plastic items that are empty, clean and dry should go in. Paper, metal, glass, food waste, liquids, tissue, e-waste, textiles and styrofoam should stay out.
follow_up_prompt: Ask me about a specific item, e.g. 'Can I recycle a green tea bottle?'

question_keywords: green tea bottle; tea bottle; can I recycle drink bottle; plastic drink bottle
user_question: Can I recycle my green tea bottle?
short_answer: Yes, if it is a plastic bottle and it is empty, clean and dry.
full_chatbot_answer: Yes. A plastic green tea bottle can go into the plastics-only bin if it is empty, clean and dry. Empty leftover tea, give it a quick rinse if sticky, let it dry, and put the plastic cap back on.
follow_up_prompt: If your bottle has a BCRS Deposit Mark, return it to a Return Right RVM if you want the 10¢ refund.

question_keywords: why clean dry; why empty clean dry; why rinse; food liquid contamination; contamination
user_question: Why must items be empty, clean and dry?
short_answer: Food and liquids contaminate recycling.
full_chatbot_answer: Food and liquids can contaminate otherwise recyclable items. Once recyclables are contaminated, they may have to be treated like general waste instead. That is why this bin uses the simple check: plastic, empty, clean and dry.
follow_up_prompt: If the item needs too much washing, general waste is usually better.

question_keywords: do I need to rinse; must rinse; wash recyclables; how clean is clean
user_question: Do I need to rinse plastic items?
short_answer: Rinse only if there is food or liquid residue.
full_chatbot_answer: If the plastic item is already empty and reasonably clean, it may not need a full wash. If there is drink, sauce, oil, food, or sticky residue, give it a quick rinse and dry it before recycling.
follow_up_prompt: If it is too oily or hard to clean, use general waste.

question_keywords: what if oily; greasy container; dirty container; food stained
user_question: What if the plastic container is oily or food-stained?
short_answer: Do not recycle it unless it can be cleaned easily.
full_chatbot_answer: Oily, greasy and food-stained containers should not go into the plastics-only bin. If a quick rinse can make it clean and dry, recycle it after cleaning. If it needs too much washing or remains greasy, use general waste.
follow_up_prompt: Ask facilities if many dirty containers are contaminating the bin.

question_keywords: cap on or off; plastic cap; bottle cap; remove cap
user_question: Should I remove the bottle cap?
short_answer: For plastic bottles, keep the plastic cap on after emptying.
full_chatbot_answer: If the cap is plastic, screw it back onto the empty, clean and dry bottle. Loose small caps can be difficult to recover.
follow_up_prompt: If the cap is metal or mixed material, keep it out of the plastics-only bin.

question_keywords: label remove; remove label; bottle label; sticker label
user_question: Do I need to remove labels from plastic bottles?
short_answer: Usually no.
full_chatbot_answer: For normal plastic bottles, labels usually do not need to be removed. The more important point is that the bottle is empty, clean and dry.
follow_up_prompt: Remove large non-plastic parts if they are easy to separate.

question_keywords: plastic number; resin code; number triangle; recycling symbol; PET HDPE PP
user_question: Do the plastic numbers mean it can be recycled?
short_answer: Not by themselves.
full_chatbot_answer: The number in the triangle is a resin identification code. It helps identify the plastic type, but it does not automatically mean the item is accepted. Use the item type, site signage and condition: plastic, empty, clean and dry.
follow_up_prompt: Tell me the item type, not just the number, for a better answer.

question_keywords: BCRS; deposit mark; return right; 10 cent refund; refund container
user_question: What should I do with a drink bottle that has a BCRS Deposit Mark?
short_answer: Return it to a Return Right RVM if you want the refund.
full_chatbot_answer: For bottles with the BCRS Deposit Mark, return the empty, uncrushed container to a Return Right RVM to receive the 10¢ refund. If you are not claiming the refund and your site allows it, the plastic bottle still needs to be empty, clean and dry before going into the plastics-only bin.
follow_up_prompt: Metal cans with deposit marks should not go into this plastics-only bin.

question_keywords: paper; cardboard; magazine; can paper go here
user_question: Can paper or cardboard go into this bin?
short_answer: No. This bin is plastics-only.
full_chatbot_answer: Paper and cardboard may be recyclable in other streams, but they should not go into this plastics-only bin. Keep this bin clean for eligible plastic items only.
follow_up_prompt: Use the paper recycling stream or blue bin if appropriate for your site.

question_keywords: metal; aluminium; aluminum; can; tin can
user_question: Can metal cans go into this bin?
short_answer: No. Use a metal recycling stream instead.
full_chatbot_answer: Metal cans may be recyclable, but they are not plastic. Do not place them in the plastics-only bin.
follow_up_prompt: If the can has a BCRS Deposit Mark, use a Return Right RVM for the refund.

question_keywords: glass; glass bottle; glass jar
user_question: Can glass go into this bin?
short_answer: No. Glass is not plastic.
full_chatbot_answer: Glass bottles and jars do not belong in the plastics-only bin. Use the correct glass recycling stream or blue bin if your site allows it.
follow_up_prompt: If broken, handle safely and report if there is a safety risk.

question_keywords: styrofoam; polystyrene; foam; foam box
user_question: Can styrofoam go into this bin?
short_answer: No.
full_chatbot_answer: Styrofoam, polystyrene, foam food boxes, foam cups, cooler boxes and packing peanuts should not go into the plastics-only bin.
follow_up_prompt: Use general waste unless your site has a dedicated foam collection.

question_keywords: soft plastic; plastic bag; bubble wrap; film
user_question: Can soft plastics go into this bin?
short_answer: Only if your site accepts soft plastics.
full_chatbot_answer: Soft plastics such as bags, bubble wrap and film are plastic, but not every plastics-only bin accepts them. Recycle them only if the bin signage or facilities team confirms soft plastics are accepted. They must still be empty, clean and dry.
follow_up_prompt: If unsure, ask facilities instead of contaminating the bin.

question_keywords: compostable; biodegradable; PLA; plant based plastic
user_question: Can compostable or biodegradable plastics go into this bin?
short_answer: No.
full_chatbot_answer: Compostable and biodegradable plastics should not go into this plastics-only recycling stream because they can contaminate conventional plastic recycling.
follow_up_prompt: Use general waste unless a dedicated compostable packaging stream is available.

question_keywords: e-waste; battery; charger; cable; electronic
user_question: Can e-waste go into this bin?
short_answer: No. Use e-waste recycling.
full_chatbot_answer: E-waste, batteries, cables, chargers and electronic items must not go into the plastics-only bin. Use dedicated e-waste recycling points or ask facilities.
follow_up_prompt: Report batteries or e-waste found in the plastics-only bin.

question_keywords: food waste; leftover food; can food go here
user_question: Can food waste go into this bin?
short_answer: No.
full_chatbot_answer: Food waste contaminates the plastics-only recycling stream. Do not place leftover food, fruit peels, tea leaves, coffee grounds or meal waste into this bin.
follow_up_prompt: Use food waste collection if available, or general waste.

question_keywords: liquid; leftover drink; drink inside; half full
user_question: What if there is liquid inside the bottle or cup?
short_answer: Empty it first.
full_chatbot_answer: Do not place liquid-filled bottles or cups into the plastics-only bin. Empty the liquid, rinse if sticky, dry the item, and only recycle it if it is accepted plastic.
follow_up_prompt: Report leaking bottles or liquid contamination.

question_keywords: takeaway container; food container; lunch box
user_question: Can takeaway plastic containers go into this bin?
short_answer: Only if clean and dry.
full_chatbot_answer: Takeaway plastic containers can go into the plastics-only bin only when all food, sauce and oil are removed and the container is dry.
follow_up_prompt: If it is greasy or hard to clean, use general waste.

question_keywords: bubble tea cup; drink cup; plastic cup
user_question: Can bubble tea cups or plastic drink cups go into this bin?
short_answer: Only if empty, clean and dry.
full_chatbot_answer: Plastic drink cups can be recycled only if all liquid, ice, pearls, toppings and residue are removed. The cup must be clean and dry.
follow_up_prompt: Straws and food-contaminated lids should go to general waste.

question_keywords: what is contamination; bin contamination; contaminated recycling
user_question: What counts as contamination?
short_answer: Anything that makes the plastics stream dirty or mixed.
full_chatbot_answer: Contamination includes food, liquids, tissue, PPE, e-waste, batteries, paper, metal, glass, styrofoam, bagged rubbish, and dirty or greasy plastic items.
follow_up_prompt: Use the report form if the bin is contaminated.

question_keywords: report issue; full bin; overflowing; bin damaged; missing bin
user_question: How do I report a bin issue?
short_answer: Use the report issue form.
full_chatbot_answer: If the bin is full, damaged, missing, leaking, contaminated, or blocked, use the report issue form so facilities or sustainability teams can follow up.
follow_up_prompt: Click 'Report bin issue' on the website.

question_keywords: blue bin; general recycling bin; same as blue bin
user_question: Is this the same as the general blue recycling bin?
short_answer: No. This is stricter.
full_chatbot_answer: Singapore’s blue recycling bins are for commingled recyclables such as paper, metal, plastic and glass, but this office bin is plastics-only. For this bin, keep non-plastic recyclables out.
follow_up_prompt: Ask facilities where paper, metal and glass should go onsite.

question_keywords: can I put all plastics; all plastic accepted; every plastic
user_question: Can all plastic items go into this bin?
short_answer: No.
full_chatbot_answer: Not all plastic items are suitable for recycling. This bin is for eligible plastic items, usually clean packaging such as bottles and containers. Keep dirty, greasy, tiny, mixed-material, foam, PPE, toys and electronics out.
follow_up_prompt: Ask about the specific item for a clearer answer.

question_keywords: why not recycle everything; wishcycle; wishcycling
user_question: Why not place doubtful items in the bin just in case?
short_answer: Because wrong items can contaminate the bin.
full_chatbot_answer: Putting uncertain items into recycling is wishcycling. It can contaminate the bin, create sorting problems, and cause otherwise recyclable plastics to be treated as waste.
follow_up_prompt: When unsure, ask or use general waste.

question_keywords: how to answer; decision rule; quick check; four checks
user_question: What is the quickest decision rule?
short_answer: Plastic, empty, clean, dry.
full_chatbot_answer: Use the four checks: Is it plastic? Is it empty? Is it clean? Is it dry? If any answer is no, do not put it into this plastics-only bin yet.
follow_up_prompt: For mixed materials, ask facilities.

question_keywords: recycle bottle with label; label on bottle
user_question: Can I recycle a bottle with the label still on?
short_answer: Yes, usually.
full_chatbot_answer: A label on a plastic bottle usually does not stop recycling. The bottle should still be empty, clean and dry.
follow_up_prompt: Remove large non-plastic attachments if easy.

question_keywords: crush bottle; flatten bottle; should I crush
user_question: Should I crush or flatten plastic bottles?
short_answer: Flatten if suitable for your bin; do not crush BCRS bottles if claiming refund.
full_chatbot_answer: Flattening can save bin space for normal plastics-only collection if your site allows it. However, if the bottle has a BCRS Deposit Mark and you want the refund, keep it uncrushed for the Return Right RVM.

question_keywords: small plastics; small items; loose caps; tiny plastic
user_question: Can small plastic items go in?
short_answer: Usually no unless attached to a larger item.
full_chatbot_answer: Small plastic items such as straws, stirrers, loose tags and small wrappers are hard to sort and should not go into the bin. Keep plastic caps attached to bottles where possible.

question_keywords: mixed material; cannot separate; paper and plastic; foil and plastic
user_question: What if the item is mixed material?
short_answer: Do not use this bin unless the plastic part can be separated.
full_chatbot_answer: Mixed-material items should not go into the plastics-only bin. If you can separate a clean plastic part, recycle only that part if accepted. If not, use general waste.

question_keywords: office pantry; pantry recycling; staff recycling; office recycling
user_question: What should staff remember for pantry recycling?
short_answer: Empty, rinse and dry plastics before recycling.
full_chatbot_answer: For pantry waste, the biggest issue is contamination from drinks, sauces, food, tissue and takeaway waste. Only place eligible plastic items in the plastics-only bin after they are empty, clean and dry.

question_keywords: what if no exact match; not listed; unknown plastic
user_question: What if my item is not listed?
short_answer: Use the four checks and be cautious.
full_chatbot_answer: If the item is not listed, recycle it only if it is clearly plastic packaging, empty, clean, dry and allowed by the site signage. If it is mixed, dirty, wet, food-stained, tiny, foam, electronic or hazardous, keep it out.

question_keywords: where does recycling go; what happens after collection; sorting; MRF
user_question: What happens after recyclables are collected?
short_answer: They are sorted at Materials Recovery Facilities.
full_chatbot_answer: Recyclables collected from Singapore’s blue bins are sent to Materials Recovery Facilities, where different material types are sorted and sent to recycling plants. For your site’s plastics-only bin, the collector may handle plastics separately depending on your contract.

question_keywords: separate plastics by number; sort plastic types; PET HDPE separate
user_question: Do I need to separate plastics by number?

question_keywords: how to improve recycling; reduce contamination; tips

short_answer: Use clear prompts and the four checks.
